title: Unlawful assembly
content: An assembly of five or more persons is designated an 'unlawful assembly', if the common object of the persons composing that assembly is: First.—To overawe by criminal force, or show of criminal force, the Federal or Provincial Government or any public servant; Second.—To resist the execution of any law, or of any legal process; Third.—To commit any mischief or criminal trespass, or other offence; Fourth.—By means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to enforce any right or supposed right; or Fifth.—By means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or to omit what he is legally entitled to do.

footnotes.text: The statutory floor: If the number of persons participating drops below five, a charge of unlawful assembly under Section 141 cannot stand, though individual liability for other offenses may still apply.

title: Rioting
content: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.

footnotes.text: The factor that upgrades an assembly to a riot. The moment even a single member uses actual physical force to achieve the group's common goal, every single person in that assembly becomes guilty of rioting.

title: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object
content: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.

footnotes.text: Constructive Liability: A pillar of criminal law. Unlike Section 34 (which requires a prior meeting of minds and active participation), Section 149 creates vicarious liability based strictly on shared membership in an unlawful assembly executing a shared objective.

title: Affray
content: When two or more persons, by fighting in a public place, disturb the public peace, they are said to 'commit an affray'.

footnotes.text: Distinction from Assault: An affray requires a minimum of two active combatants fighting each other in a location accessible to the public, resulting in an actual disturbance of public peace.
